record.description:
In this three-part interview, Ermestine Martin describes her experiences growing up in Checotah, Oklahoma and moving to Richmond, California during the war years. Throughout these interviews, Martin explores the impact of racism and finding strength in her relationships as she developed her career in real estate.
The first interview is focused on Martin’s exploration of her family history and her childhood working on the family farm. Due to years of crop failure as a result of the drought, Martin’s family eventually moved to Richmond, California. She describes what life was like in Richmond after the war, living in a public housing community, and how her father opened his own business. In the second interview, Martin discusses how the war impacted her family, her views on war, and how her community found strength and hope in the soldiers returning home. Martin explains her thoughts on the importance and value of education and work, and describes her first jobs working in a factory and as a care-giver before becoming involved in the real estate industry. The third interview is focused on Martin’s career as part of a group of African American realtors, and explores her relationships with her family and coworkers as she worked to desegregate Richmond’s residential neighborhoods in a post-war era.
record.transcript:
WILMOT: Recording. Recording. Okay, good morning Mrs. Martin.
MARTIN: Good morning.
WILMOT: Today is, let us see, today is April 5, 2005, interview two, and this is Ermestine Martin. I wanted to start off today by asking you, was there anything that you wanted to add, that you had forgotten, or remembered over the last week, from our last conversation, talking about your family background? Anything that came to you?
MARTIN: I can't recall, other than I think I had talked about the early experience in school, and then moved up to my entering into college, and then our trip to California, or change of residence to live in California.
WILMOT: I remember you telling me that you came on the train, is that right?
MARTIN: No, we had the same car I drove to high school, we drove that to California, which was a 1940 Chevrolet.
WILMOT: And how long, do you remember how many days you were on the road?
MARTIN: It was possibly--really I don't know, maybe three.
WILMOT: And do you remember, did you stay in friends' houses overnight, or did you just drive through the night?
MARTIN: No, we stayed in the car, we were in the car all the way. It probably wasn't three days, it probably was two. There were two drivers, because my mother didn't drive, but my dad drove and I was his relief driver.
WILMOT: You talked to me a little bit about where you lived, and how you moved a couple of times when you first came to Richmond. I wanted to ask you a little bit more about your father's work. You said he, as you recall, he got a job at the shipyards. Do you remember anything, can you tell me a little bit more about his union membership? Was there, what do you know about that?
MARTIN: I really did not know much about the actual facility or his meetings or how he joined, how much it cost. I know he became a union member, and as a construction person he worked in possibly cleaning up or some of the final things at the shipyards. As a member of a union.
WILMOT: Do you remember what shipyard he was at?
MARTIN: That was three.
WILMOT: Do you remember him? Did he ever tell you stories about what went on there and so forth?
MARTIN: No I don't remember him telling me stories about the shipyard. I just don't recall any conversation exactly of what was going on, but I'm pretty sure the ship building had ceased and that there was some maintenance and cleaning up, or some other work for construction or a person to come in and continue to work in that area of shipyard three.
WILMOT: Did he ever bring people home from work?
MARTIN: No.
WILMOT: And did you recall your parents sort of building friendships as they got there, making new friends? You mentioned that had already had friends that were from Oklahoma.
MARTIN: It appears to me as we came in they had--the shipyards were over and they had gone back to Oklahoma when we came in. I kind of admired my father for the strength of coming, and just taking a strong stance and came on here even though the shipyards were closing. He felt confident that he could make it here. And he did.
WILMOT: Will you tell me a little bit more then, about how he opened his own business?
MARTIN: Well, before that he worked for, let's see, he opened his business because in construction there was something, an area called Canal. There was an area that was vacant in the midst of that that wasn't built up with those housing facilities there. As he was cleaning and doing whatever they did from construction standpoint that he thought, it was a little house, a little shack, like, he called it "the shack." But anyway he decided he wanted--it's kind of amazing to know that he could see through the fact that all these people were living in these little houses, in the government housing which was quite congested. He thought he could make it by selling, well, it was really ice cream and soda and candy bars and that kind of thing. My mother thought he should have kept his job, she didn't think that was what he should do.
WILMOT: So he didn't even take it on as a second job, he really put all his energy into it.
MARTIN: He just advised us that he was going to open up this little Kiss Shack. He rented the space from someone who was kind of in charge of that area of cleaning up. It was kind of like, it was surrounded, the housing projects were kind of around it, surrounded, it was sort of in the middle of it. He had a good drawing for customers from all those housing units in the Canal district.
WILMOT: Were there other stores also?
MARTIN: Not then. It was just his little venture. He was sort of, it was kind of strange how all that happened, because I asked Dad, why did he buy one of the pieces of land and build his own store. My dad said, Thelma, the people he was renting from, said that the Japanese were going to come back and claim that land, it wasn't a good idea. Because I can remember when I'd drive Dad to Martinez to see about it, but this woman, he was more confident in what she was saying than in what I thought. Again, I was probably nineteen, maybe barely twenty years old. He didn't have the confidence in me as he did the woman that--in fact, it was a pretty bad deal because he ventured in on a 50 percent profit. I think she wouldn't charge him rent, she wanted 50 percent of the profit and we did all the work, I think my dad started out with his own money, he didn't need any money to get his items that he was going to sell. That was her way of renting it to him on a percentage. It seems like it was 50 percent of the profit. Then, what really was interesting and I always feel kind of bad that I didn't really force it or go into Martinez myself and try to look into that, of buying a piece of that land--what happened later, she bought a piece of the land in that area and opened a store and he worked for them. That wasn't really--he didn't feel comfortable in it and then he went back to construction work.
WILMOT: I was adjusting the volume when you were telling me about the family, or the people who rented to your father, Thelma. I'm sorry, was she black?
MARTIN: She was Caucasian.
WILMOT: She was white. And then, okay. Also, I wanted to ask this question. There was this property in Martinez that was formerly, formerly belonged to Japanese Americans and then they were interned? Is that how that was?
MARTIN: No, we were here in the Canal apartment area, right near Cutting, almost to Point Richmond is where that housing project was, which also, the shipyards were in the same area. That was the closest housing project to shipyard three, is the Canal apartments. And Thelma--there was an area that I think, I don't know, they didn't build the houses that we were living in in that particular area. For some reason, they were cleaning it up and Dad discovered this little area and he thought he wanted to venture into that. But he didn't--when I asked him to go to Martinez, that's the county records for here, for this county, is in Martinez, and that's where we would have to go to find out the owners of the land to make some contact. He just said, "Well, Thelma said the Japs, the Japanese are going to come back and take over."
WILMOT: But she bought it.
MARTIN: But she bought it, or she was into buying that piece of land, opening up a store. That always stuck with me. You learn by your experiences. So when they opened up a big store it was all very close to the Kiss Shack, well that was the death of the Kiss Shack. Then he started working for them and he didn't really like that, working for them and he went back to construction. He was, as I said, a very good--into the farm and all the things you had to do there, which was construction as well as you have to maintain. You have to do a lot of things. He got a job with Nora Davis in El Cerrito near Sunset cemetery. She built elaborate homes all around the cemetery. He stayed with her a lot of years.
WILMOT: Doing construction?
MARTIN: No, he worked in the landscaping with her. Her husband was an architect and she was a landscape architect. He did the--with her supervision he did all the landscaping. It's the homes that are surrounding a part of the cemetery in El Cerrito.
WILMOT: Wow.
MARTIN: So he stayed there until he retired.
WILMOT: You have really strong roots to this area.
MARTIN: Yes.
WILMOT: I mean, in addition to the other places you have roots to.
MARTIN: Yes.
WILMOT: During this time, did your mother stay--was she making a home? Was she a homemaker or did she ever enter the work force?
MARTIN: She worked with some people, ladies at the church, at--there was a fish cannery locally here. It's maybe near the shipyards. Somewhere in that area there was a fish packing canning company, cannery, and she worked there for a while and that was the extent of the work she did in California. Dad was the provider.
WILMOT: Mm-hmm. You also had a brother, he was in the military?
MARTIN: He was in the navy, construction battalion. They called them CBs, construction battalion.
WILMOT: And did you also have a sister, is that correct?
MARTIN: Yes, I had a sister.
WILMOT: So these two, during that time, what were they up to? Were you getting letters from your brother in the military telling you about all his exciting places he was seeing?
MARTIN: It seems to me it was hard to hear from him. You just didn't hear like--I don't know what was going on, but you didn't hear from them very often. I had a friend from Oklahoma that graduated I think two years before I, and he was the closest male to me, and I couldn't hear from him often. I didn't hear, we didn't hear from my brother that often. We finally got a letter once when he was injured and he received a purple heart.
WILMOT: Was he injured in battle?
MARTIN: Yes, it was battle. The story from him was that he dug, you have to do a foxhole, and he had the foxhole and a young man from our same area in Oklahoma, Joe LeCour didn't dig a foxhole. I think he thought that this probably never happened. But when this attack came, they were to run to the foxhole, and this man, he shared his foxhole. Therefore his arm was raised, on the side, and that's where--I don't know how to pronounce is, sapplin {shrapnel?} or something, it's a part of a shell, it hit his arm and that's where he was injured.
WILMOT: Scrapple?
MARTIN: Is it scrapple? I'm not certain. Scapplin.
WILMOT: He was injured and took that injury forward into his life. Did he recover?
MARTIN: He didn't, it didn't affect him later in life.
WILMOT: So when it was time--when he came back from the war, did he tell you about his experiences in the war?
MARTIN: Well that was one major one, how he--he was sharing his foxhole. Therefore, instead of being on his back or his belly, on his stomach--I imagine it's maybe the stomach that you would lie on--
WILMOT: Shrapnel?
MARTIN: Shrapnel, that's it. Shrapnel. He was on his side, to share this. I guess both of them, their arms, part was up. Shrapnel?
WILMOT: Shrapnel.
MARTIN: Shrapnel struck him there.
WILMOT: It's odd though to think though that someone who was part of a construction battalion that he actually got caught in cross fire. I wonder how that happened?
MARTIN: Well, construction, my brother was into record keeping, book keeping, something like that, he wasn't exactly firing but they were all there together. I'm assuming that they were--all of this was necessary, someone there to protect, and then some to keep the records, or order the--make communications, something of that nature.
I didn't get--there I go again. [weeping]
WILMOT: [retrieves tissue] I do have another question about that. Let me just put this here. Which is about--was this, LeCour, was he the same person who you knew who was also in World War II?
MARTIN: No I had a special friend, what I thought was always my boyfriend, all my life--you know, as a kid coming up, we'd exchange gifts and, he was the one when I told you before was smart, I thought? His mother had the general store and he would be up--I could hear the train and I could think, "That's the train, he's putting his mail up on the train." Making the connection, without the train stopping because it was a small black town called Rentiesville, Oklahoma. It's one of the few black towns in Oklahoma.
WILMOT: So the train wouldn't stop? So it would slow and you'd put the mail on it?
MARTIN: It seems to me that was the story, I didn't ever see it happen, but I didn't think it went to a full stop in Rentiesville, but the mail was able to be connected to the train.
But LeCour was just someone who lived near Checotah. He was, I can remember, when I was back in Oklahoma on a visit, it was kind of like something he always remembered, is that experience with the foxhole.
Your question about my brother being there and was he actually in battle, I just imagine that they had some of various people working there, it was a battalion of people and they needed possibly cooks, and they probably needed people who kept up with the ammunition and the firing and that, and others who took care of communication. I thought he was like a bookkeeper or something like that.
WILMOT: When he came out of the war, did he come to California? Did he come to Richmond?
MARTIN: He did, he came right to that Canal apartment, which was very close to shipyard.
WILMOT: Did you know he was coming?
MARTIN: It seems like that was sort of a surprise. That's what I'm saying, you didn't get the communication that you would like to have gotten, a letter every day, or, it seemed like months, two months or so or maybe longer and you wouldn't hear from your people in the military. Probably different now, you have so many ways of sending emails and all kinds of things.
WILMOT: That actually brings me to some other questions. I think I began to ask you about this last time, which was, how do you think the war that's happening now is different than, or similar--are the war times the same, World War II and now Iraq?
MARTIN: I'm not sure. I really didn't know, like when the war came, history and things that I'd studied, it never occurred to me about another country. I thought that--I just didn't have any sense of what was really going on when all at once we were going--the fighting started. It was around school that young men graduating from high school would be leaving and going to war. This friend of mine also was going, but he was actually shipping out of the Los Angeles area, I think it was Point Juanima or something like that. Have you heard of that? He was shipping out of there. My brother went to the South Pacific theater of war, and served there. I think that was the only area that he served.
WILMOT: So when you think about how war is happening right now, are the two war times different? Do they feel different to you?
MARTIN: Oh of course. Because now, I've met a lot of people from all over the world. At that time as I said before, in number one conversation, that I was kind of limited to my area, and the world was not that important to me. Just like that's what they were talking about in history, but I'll never see it, I'll never trod that soil, I'll never go to Europe--that I've done, I've been to Germany and France and places. Like my dad would maybe talk about World War I when he was a cook in World War I and that he was in France, and he would say something about the cheese had maggots in it and they would eat it. I couldn't get that together. Plus he would say, "Poule vouz France," he would say a few words in French, because he was a cook. How do you pronounce it?
WILMOT: I don't know, I'm not sure if he's saying, "Parlez vouz Francais?"
MARTIN: Parlez vouz Francais! He would say that. And some other words because of his experience as a cook there for the military.
WILMOT: Did he tell you anything else about his experience there?
MARTIN: He talked about Chillicothe, Ohio, and when they came out. I can't remember the story, I just remember, that's where they were dismissed from the army, in Chillicothe, Ohio. I didn't remember how they got from there to Checotah, Oklahoma. Probably train, I guess, or something.
WILMOT: So it felt like he was talking about a life that all happened before marriage and family.
MARTIN: No, he had been married before, and he had a family, as I said. He was twenty years older than my mom, and he had a family of two children. That marriage dissolved when he was overseas. When he was overseas that all vanished. The wife had another child I think when he got back home. [laughter] He was gone two years and she had a new baby or something and that kind of dissolved that marriage. Then I don't know how he met my mom, but I understand he proposed in my grandfather's orchard. [laughter]
WILMOT: You told me that last time, I love that story.
MARTIN: That was that.
WILMOT: Well, as far as World War II, in the 1940s was there the sense around you and amongst your family and friends that this was a just war, that people were fighting for a good cause?
MARTIN: You know, the cause didn't seem to ever--I didn't seem to get into that very much. It seems like all I would ever--you know what I think happened? I think it's like these songs we were singing were so pretty and you had a life of, "They'll be home again," "All over the world." [sings] Do you know some of the old songs we sang in those days?
WILMOT: When you say they'll be home again, all over the world, do you mean that the soldiers will come back?
MARTIN: Maybe that was World War II when we were singing those songs, but not World War I. But World War II, we had such beautiful love songs. You don't know them?
WILMOT: No.
MARTIN: [sings] "When the lights go on again, all over the world, and we'll have wedding rings," so we had this big imagination. God, it was beautiful. Then we had another, we had some kids like--again, we used to say. Let me see, there was a song about, 4-F, "You're the only thing that's left, but you will do until my G.I. Joe comes home." [laughter]
WILMOT: No! Uh-uh! [laughter]
MARTIN: That was all that was there. But still we had the imagination of coming home. You see, you will do until the G.I. Joe comes home. All over the world. So we had this great imagination, we thought they were coming home, and it was just a feeling that you had. [sings] "When the lights go on again, all over the world." Did you ever hear that one?
WILMOT: Hm-mm.
MARTIN: You haven't heard that one?!
WILMOT: The first time I heard it is you telling me now. It's so livable. {?}
MARTIN: [sings again] "When lights go on again, all over the world, and we'll have things like wedding rings, and free hearts will sing." Something like that. So when you had all of this, you really--and you were just hoping and praying that you'd be alright and you had confidence in our people, that they were going to win, make it. You knew they were coming home, that's the way we sang songs. It was just like, and then they were coming home, and then that's why we had what we calls these babies now, what your papa--
WILMOT: Baby boomers.
MARTIN: Baby boomers! [laughter] It really happened, they came home and they had baby booms, and they were building houses like they are today. You know, building houses for the baby boomers. Some people had someone they were waiting for, did come home, some of them were already married perhaps. But usually the younger guys, people that I knew, left right out of high school, so they missed a lot. They missed a lot of--you know, they didn't go in as an older person. That's why all the women and people were kind of waiting on these younger guys to come home. We certainly, I think, thought they were going to come home. At least I did.
WILMOT: I want to ask, did people come back changed?
MARTIN: Yeah, I think my brother was changed. I think he was changed some. Some people were injured, mentally injured, until they were not able to handle themselves, shell-shocked and all that stuff they'd gone through. It's painful and it has a lasting effect on some people. I thought my dad must have came out okay, when he went through--probably as you said before, he wasn't exactly fighting, he was a cook. He was a real nice cook, he could cook things even until--like, I have canned fruit that did. He had a beautiful garden here in Richmond, and he would make preserve, he had tomato preserve with lemon in it, and I still have a jar of it. He must have done that for me, well, he gave it to me probably in 1970 something and I still have it.
WILMOT: Did you learn how to cook from your father or your mother?
MARTIN: Well, I really didn't learn--I don't know, I guess both. My father was a cook that--my mother used a cookbook, and if it said half a teaspoon, she would take the teaspoon and take a knife and exactly get the half. If it said a level teaspoon--I don't know why they say level teaspoon--she would take a knife and make it level. But her food was never as delicious as my dad's, I didn't think.
WILMOT: He used intuition.
MARTIN: I guess he did. [laughter] but my mother's food was nice. She did some things much better. Like candied yams, they were so glazed and pretty and whole yet soft and delectable. Her chocolate cake and coconut cake--she was so exact that her foods were just, you know, come out real nice. For Christmas that's what we had, a coconut cake and chocolate cake. But you didn't touch it until Christmas day. In those days they would make a cake, usually I think before, not Christmas day, you wouldn't think of that, you know, a day or so before. And you get to look at that and want to taste it so badly. We would be able to take the little remainings in the mixing bowl, and then be able to take a spoon and scrape that off, get that much of it ahead of time. [laughter]
WILMOT: I wanted to ask you a little bit more about the war time, which is, did people in the Bay Area, and yourself, and people you knew, did you have a sense of what was happening with the Nazis and the Jewish people in the concentration camps? Was there a sense that people knew that that was happening?
MARTIN: Well I was not--it was a sense of, did I really know all about the happenings of--
WILMOT: The genocide.
MARTIN: No, I really didn't. It seemed to be a sense that that was--I didn't understand so much about how the United States is getting involved and all of that, in my opinion, I guess, I thought why in the world would we get involved in anything if they're going to take our children. Especially people my age, at that time, at my age level, would be losing all their boyfriends, they were all gone. Any young man you had in mind was gone away. As I said, nothing left but--
WILMOT: 4-F? 4-H?
MARTIN: 4-F. [sings] "You're the only thing that's left, but you'll do--" And there were some guys that went and--they really just cleaned all the boys out, away. So you would probably not have a chance to really get a whole lot of sympathy with what's happening to another country when you're losing your best. Except the 4-Fs, you know. I think that's evident--oh, I guess that's how they graded them. You get an F or something, but then you get four and so you stay there. Usually they had, I don't know what they had, I know one particular person someone had said he had venereal disease as a child going around school, and it was said, all the people would kind of--and he was left, so I guess he might have been a 4-F. He was, because he didn't go with the rest of the kids. So what we were hearing probably was true, he had something. He looked like the rest of the boys in a sense. But he had some little darkness right around the bottom of his nose, and some of the kids would say, "That's what that is," because when they get--I don't know where they got that.
WILMOT: They were cruel.
MARTIN: Yes.
WILMOT: I wonder, when did the information about the concentration camps start to break? When did people start to learn about what was going on in Germany?
MARTIN: The way it became important to me is when Thelma told my dad that the Japanese were going to come back and claim that land. You know what I'm saying? Other than that, I heard it, but I think--I really think that we were more involved in our young men being safe and coming home, and other peoples' problem was not even as severe as ours in a sense. It was probably more severe, but in a way we didn't know they were coming back. We had all this desire and we had imagination, dreams, and just know they are coming back. If you could remember the songs that we were singing at that time. I don't remember the one about women working in the military. But I remember any songs about what we were doing as women. Like when I worked at the ammunition depot.
WILMOT: I wanted to ask you also, I realize now--was there, I believe the disappearance of the Japanese into the concentration camps happened before you got to California.
MARTIN: Oh yes.
WILMOT: So you didn't, you probably didn't experience--were there Japanese in Oklahoma? Did you experience any of that kind of disappearance?
MARTIN: Not in my area.
WILMOT: Not in your area. And did you have an awareness of that happening, that Japanese people were being put in concentration camps?
MARTIN: Oh I heard it, but as I said, I think that was possibly painful in a sense, but what we were going through was just as--they were in a concentration camp, but they were safe. We were taking reasonable care of them I suppose. But you think of sending our children, you can't exactly--I don't think one can put what it sounds like to you, how bad it was.
WILMOT: I think I was getting confused because at first I was talking about the Jewish people in Germany and then I was talking about the Japanese people here. I was asking about both. Then I switched back and forth. I didn't mean to ask two questions at the same time.
MARTIN: Oh, I was just thinking about the Japanese and I was thinking about when the woman told my dad. That's when it occurred to me a little about them, that when I hear something about it it became very interesting. But maybe before then, it just really--see, there again, my brother and my friend and other schoolmates, and cousins and things, were in service. That was so deep on you, you hardly have any room to go anywhere with other people.
WILMOT: Mm-hmm.
MARTIN: I mean, you do, but you're loaded already. You've got more on your plate than you can handle with your own people going to war, not knowing why they're going, really. Just that they were told to go and they're eighteen years old and they go to Muskogee, Oklahoma, and you catch something and go to--I don't know, it was Fort Seal, perhaps, Fort Seal, Oklahoma.
WILMOT: When you, in looking at the war we're in now, how does it feel? Does it feel the same as back in 1940, World War II?
MARTIN: Well, it can't feel--I don't think it feels the same. There's nothing could feel as--to me, like that did, because we were losing--well, other people here are losing their relatives. But in my case, those were my brother and my friends, and so forth that were going away. That immediate situation was right in your heart, whereas, what's happening in another country, you have a long distance between that and yourself. Then, too, right now, when other people are losing their lives and our young men, it's back to the same thing. It's hard to get over into that when we're losing our own people here, from here.
WILMOT: Do you think we're at war for a good reason right now?
MARTIN: Do I think we're in war for a good reason? Let me see. I think that biblically speaking, no. If I'm going to use a--I just did two skits for Easter. When you go through all that you wonder. But they had wars and rumors of war then, so it's inevitable that we're going to have them. Then again, when you think of that, you think there's going to be wars. When you grow up to that, the more I read the Bible, and search it--I just picked up a third grade book at the store, "Every third grade should know." When I read, when I was looking at my third-grader and my second grade child, I was wondering what they should know. Much of it was Bible, for history. In the history studies there they talked about before Christ and after Christ. They had something like a graph, showing Alexander the Great, it was kind of like--I said, "Third graders?" Then I looked at that, there were things I really didn't kind of know when Alexander the Great came in and when other people--you know, what happened before there was no world at all. When there was nothing there, how did, how it begun and how it moved up, and before Christ, events that were happening. I wasn't that clear on them. I want to now, so this week I'm going to go out and go over that, because many third graders don't know what that particular person who produced that book for third graders, I don't think many kids, students, know that. At least, I was learning from the book.
WILMOT: That happens frequently. I wonder when you say biblically, no, this is not a just war; I'm putting words in your mouth, let me say exactly. I asked you do you think we're at war for a good reason and you said biblically no. What do you mean when you say biblically no?
MARTIN: Well, I was saying then, and I changed to say well, the Bible says there will be wars and rumors of wars. So then I kind of erased that a little bit, saying as I went to the Bible, and whether it's just or not it's already predicted, there'll be wars and rumors of wars. What I've found out in my last, in my past fifty years, I really have gotten a little bit more interested in history, which when you think of the Bible is quite accurate history, I think. What has happened, what's supposed to happen, what's going to happen, is fulfilling, whatever you read there and you think about it, it's really happening, you know? I guess it was always that way, like families not getting along and that kind of thing.
WILMOT: Okay, I hear you. As far as the actual reasons why we're at war in Iraq, which ostensibly were about weapons of mass destruction, do you think that was--and 9/11 and terrorism--do you think we have good reason to be over there?
MARTIN: I guess I don't know, it could be a good reason, but it seems like they're not successful. They're not successful, it might be a false accusation maybe or something, I don't know. Because they said these weapons were there and all of this, and in fact it seems like we didn't have correct information or we weren't smart enough. We're supposed to be intelligent about what is going on and that didn't happen. If they had been successful in their trials there I would think one way. But they haven't been able to find anything that I have heard. Have you heard of them finding something?
WILMOT: No.
MARTIN: It seems like a joke, in a sense. It seems like we just weren't successful, which is sort of embarrassing.
WILMOT: Do you know anyone over there right now who is fighting?
MARTIN: No, I just know one of our young men at our church is back now, he left high school, and he played the drums in our church and his family moved while he was still in service to Texas. He is back home, his grandmother told me last week that he was back. He was a helicopter--he left here, this area, going to somewhere back east to train in the helicopter field. Then he became a helicopter person. But he survived it. Some others in our church, not many, that I've heard, came out successful. We pray every Sunday, you know, when it first started and so forth, that was kind of in every general prayer, was the boys, and they would name some of them. So far, I haven't heard of any of them, any casualties of that. But our church is small. I haven't heard of many casualties from Richmond. Not that I know--I don't know that many people.
WILMOT: I wanted to ask you also then, we have another about fifteen minutes left on this tape, so I was thinking maybe we would go back to the 1940s. After your brother got out of the military and came to Richmond, what kind of work did he find? Did he work immediately? Or did he kind of recuperate and rest?
MARTIN: No, he got a job at Swift's packing company, I think it was Swift. Swift's packing company in San Francisco. And he stayed there until he passed. He died at thirty-eight years old.
WILMOT: He was young.
MARTIN: But he stayed there. His heart was enlarged. He just stayed there. My mother, she wanted him to go to college, but somehow he seemed to have been doing okay with what he was doing.
WILMOT: Did he live with your family or did he live in San Francisco?
MARTIN: No, he lived with us while working until he married. Then he was back home at the time he died, he was staying home with my father and mother. My dad used to do his breakfast every morning. [laughter] I'd think, "Golly he has it easy," because I had a family and then work. Dad would get up and fix his breakfast. I think that's why he kept coming back home. He'd go away and marry and separate and he'd come in to live with my mother and father.
WILMOT: To get breakfast. [laughter]
MARTIN: Yes. No, he lived with them as he broke up from his family. He just had a wife, he didn't have any children.
WILMOT: Your sister, was she working at this time?
MARTIN: When we came to California?
WILMOT: Yes, in California.
MARTIN: No, she was in high school.
WILMOT: She was young.
MARTIN: Yes, not that young, but she was in high school. She didn't really like to do a lot of work. So she married someone quite a few years older than she. He was really a hard worker and kind of an entrepreneur kind of guy, he started this black trucking company here, kind of. He was kind of like the dean of the black truckers because he started buying big diesel trucks and hauling for people, like the lumber industry. He would go up to Eureka and bring in lumber, he would haul Christmas trees at Christmas time, huge ones for some place, public place. Then his last job was Pacific Steel Supply, is where he was working. I think it was Pacific Steel, Jutsons Steel, one steel company is where he passed. He died there. Passed out and that was the end. But they knew. The hospital had told him not to go back, and he slipped out of the hospital and got in his trucks drove to the end. That was my sister's husband. He was quite a provider for his family. He had two children, he'd been married before with no children, and with her they had a boy and a girl. He was contented, very proud to have these kids, because he was, I guess, forty when they were born, forty-something years old.
WILMOT: He was very pleased, he was proud?
MARTIN: Yes. His boy was Larry, little Larry. His boy took the trucking industry and kept it until a few years ago. His father died when he was eighteen or nineteen and he started with that. He just took over. He'd be working with him in the summers, and be in the trucks washing them.
WILMOT: This is your nephew and your niece, your nephew.
MARTIN: It was my nephew, yes.
WILMOT: Also, during that time, was there a thought for you about going back to school?
MARTIN: When?
WILMOT: When you came to California, and you were working, were you thinking about going back to school? Was that on your mind?
MARTIN: I did. I was always in school, all my life. I've got a course going usually somewhere. One of these other kinds of courses. Now they have so many courses that are--I'm going to do one on cell sites in Sacramento on the 12th.
WILMOT: What is that? Cell sites.
MARTIN: Cell sites. I have cell sites on this building and I don't know anything about it and when they came here to negotiate I didn't know how to negotiate it. In the real estate profession it's kind of new. Cell sites are new to most people. To all people, for the last general--
WILMOT: Does that mean you're selling part of a house?
MARTIN: No, it's just a--a cell site is this building here, has a sell sites. It's a tall building, it's the tallest in the area, so they get signals over. You know? Signals, like Sprint, Sprint has a cell site here.
WILMOT: Oh, so you sell part--
MARTIN: I don't sell anything. This is what they call a cell phone, and for this thing to function--
WILMOT: Oh, okay, I didn't understand. I was confused.
MARTIN: You're thinking about selling something.
WILMOT: I was thinking sales site, and then I thought sell site, but I didn't understand, it's the way you can leverage part of your property.
MARTIN: It's just a necessity that they have to raise the heights as high as they can in a locale to get he signals clear. To get the signals going right, they have to get their sites up to a reasonable height. I don't know why I said that. You asked--
WILMOT: I had asked you a question about when you came here after the war with your family--
MARTIN: Did I go to school.
WILMOT: Yes, was school on the horizon for you? Was it something you were interested in and was an opportunity available to you? You mentioned you were taking a class on cell sites.
MARTIN: Yes, I always go to school. I didn't have the money just to, say, go to school, because I had to work and all my dad and mother came here-- and I always had a book in hand and a job. My dad was older then but he still worked. I don't know, it's the way you have to survive I guess. I couldn't exactly expect my dad and mother to send me to school. Even then, when I first went to college at Langston, I didn't ask them for anything. But I like to go to school because I like to know what's going on. I've been in music school the last five years.
WILMOT: You can tell from hearing you sing.
MARTIN: I'm not right now. I'm going there after school--I could get that together if I wasn't under this--as you do history and go back, it's not that easy to talk about. Some parts of it, some parts are okay. But you really kind of reminisce. Some people it doesn't bother, I guess. Schooling is important, and to work and go to school, or take a class, or two classes, one class, whatever. With what you learn if you are working in most any field plus what you learn in school to me is a better connection than just going to school. If you can gain some experiences, even a child going to say work in a grocery store as a bagger, he's ahead of a child that doesn't go. Or if he goes to work in any company, if he just takes a construction job, he'd probably be a better engineer than the one that didn't. He knows what it's all about. He probably can tell some of the teachers something. Because if a teacher has never actually walked that walk in any field he's read what he's read, similar to what you asked me about the war. I had never had any experience with it, but I did get the experience of my loved ones and neighbors and things going to war. We talked about them all the time. Other people throughout the world was not really our daily conversation, but anywhere you'd go, mothers had ribbons and things. Still now they do, that's self--self preservation is the first law of nature. The other part of the world is important, but you certainly have to take care of what's so close to you. You couldn't even hardly jump through, away from that part to get over there. You can, but golly, you've got so many hurts. Like the girls here, the women overseas, you hear that, and you hear about some other person in another country being killed, and then our people--it's again, self preservation.
WILMOT: I understand that you went on to marry someone who was from another country, is that correct?
MARTIN: Mm-hmm.
WILMOT: Did that kind of change your world view?
MARTIN: Yeah, not really. He was from Jamaica, and they too were sort of different from Oklahoma. They had quite a few ways that--I know that when we would wear certain kinds of dress clothes, my husband's father, he would dress like what we would dress like, he would wear English suits, the kind of tweed. Nice wool, heavy nice fabric, simple suit, and the English-like shoes, when he would dress. He worked at Atchison Village. Before he brought them here he was a seaman, he was a sailor.
WILMOT: Okay. I feel like I didn't get to finish asking you a little bit more about your education, but then I'll move into the next part of your life after that. Just quickly, so you did--did you take classes in those first couple of years or did you wait a while to get acclimated to this place? What kind of classes were you taking?
MARTIN: Actually, I took, I was working and taking a beauty course. In fact, I was taking cosmetology in the evenings at Langston University.
WILMOT: In addition to your full--
MARTIN: I had a full load, made straight A in chemistry. In the evenings, because I didn't have to work, even in the dormitory, but I had extra time. So I would be with--Mrs. Berry was a cosmetology teacher at Langston. So I finished the cosmetology while working at a job in San Francisco.
WILMOT: Did you ever think to go into cosmetology?
MARTIN: I worked a while in that. I kept my license. But that just didn't do for me what my--I liked the real estate stuff. The land, the houses, the something, it just overpowered cosmetology for me. I think it's because I'm not--you asked me once, was I popular.
WILMOT: I asked you that question.
MARTIN: I think a beautician has to be kind of popular.
WILMOT: And talkative.
MARTIN: Yes, and all of that. My mind would be--that's just not quite what I'd like to do.
WILMOT: That popularity question is a funny question. I'm glad you called me on it.
MARTIN: What?
WILMOT: That it's a funny question.
MARTIN: Why?
WILMOT: Just--
MARTIN: I don't think so. There are people who were popular. My husband was. My husband was so very popular, and you have to be a certain kind of person.
WILMOT: Petgrave or Martin? Which one are you referring to?
MARTIN: Martin was the real popular--they're both Leos though and I didn't know until they passed. One passed in '96 in December and the other one in February of '97. They both were Leos and they did do some things for me, it was the same. I wanted him to go to {Mylans?} and get me a watch, my first husband. He went to this, Mylans, which is half a block from here, it used to be. He bought the best watch in the store. I didn't like that, because you know, we were paying back his father for our first house. I said to him, "If we're going to get married, we need to get the house." So he borrowed $2,000 from his father.
WILMOT: In Jamaica?
MARTIN: No, his father was here then. He brought the children here and he stayed here. His father was here working at Atchison Village, right down, not far from the shipyards.
WILMOT: So your first husband was from Richmond.
MARTIN: My first husband was from Kingston, Jamaica. He was born there. His father married a Cuban woman, and he was also a sailor. While he was out at sea and sailing around the world his wife died. Her name was Clara Petgrave, and she left three children. She passed and one of the children passed. He sent for his children to come to Richmond by boat, and they came. So he stayed here. His wife and other child was dead, and he had these two here. There was a woman we called Aunt Ada, that I thought was their aunt, but it wasn't, it was just like a friend that helped him with those children, the two children. He was a Jamaican by birth, his birthright, and was there until I guess six years old or something like that.
WILMOT: Did he have an accent?
MARTIN: His father did, not much. They talked about things like an automobile, he'd talk about the machine, and the only machine I knew about was a sewing machine, or like you know, a sewing machine. Usually, he just--an automobile was--some things he used was different than what I would use.
WILMOT: Different words, I understand. How did you meet?
MARTIN: A friend of mine that I met, I also worked at the post office, and I met this girl, and she was a big one on birthdays. Socially, I'm not that social, so I didn't really have many birthday parties. She found out that my birthday was in May and one year she gave me a little dinner at her house. The next year she called me and said, "You know, I want you to come over tomorrow, you know what tomorrow is?" I said, "Oh, no, what?" She said, "It's your birthday." I said, "Oh, yes." I went to dinner at her home. She had a cake and a nice dinner. They lived in another housing project up around upper, near El Cerrito, Bayview? No, it wasn't Bayview. Anyway, I went up there and she said, "Oh, I see Mrs. Allison's brother out there, you mind if he comes to dinner?" I said, "No I don't mind."
WILMOT: And how old were you at this birthday?
MARTIN: I don't know, I guess I was about twenty.
WILMOT: So this was just shortly after you got here.
MARTIN: It wasn't that long. I started to work as soon as I--and that's how I met her.
WILMOT: So it was like a year, you met him a year after you got here.
MARTIN: I was, I don't know, maybe twenty-one. But I know that he came to dinner, and while we were eating dinner or somewhere in that area, in that period of time, we started to discuss Billy Estine you ever hear of Billy Estine?
WILMOT: Hm-mm.
MARTIN: You never heard? He probably had one of those love songs, too. He came for dinner, and then we talked about going to the Fillmore Theater, no, there was a place that they had dances and concerts on Fillmore Street. We asked him about, well, I don't know, she probably asked him about going and we went. I didn't know him. He said yes we would go, and we rode, he had a new Plymouth.
WILMOT: Was he older than you?
MARTIN: Let me see. Four years I guess.
WILMOT: A little bit.
MARTIN: Yes. We got ready to go, and we got in his car, and she got in the center, in the middle, when we opened the door. See, she knew him, I didn't know him. I opened the door and somehow she got in. Then we went, and it was a beautiful concert by Billy Estine, quite a singer. On the way back, she was getting in the car, and he asked her if it would be alright to let me sit in the center. [laughter] I was shocked in a way, but I didn't pay much attention to it. From then on we were pretty close, from then on. It was surprising to me, because I wasn't thinking about that, I was just going out to have a good time. Billy Estine, he was so famous and just, was just such a right-on musician.
WILMOT: What was your husband's first name?
MARTIN: The first one?
WILMOT: The first and last name of your first husband.
MARTIN: Isaac Ezekiel Petgrave.
WILMOT: Thank you. I'm going to pause here.
[interview interruption; exchange recording media; begin audio file 3]
WILMOT: We're back with our second tape of the interview. So when you came to the Bay Area you were working for NBC in San Francisco.
MARTIN: That's my first job.
WILMOT: How long were you there?
MARTIN: They went on strike. I was there a good while, they went on strike and I guess I didn't stay home. I went to an employment agency and got a job working with a woman, as a person in charge of her child. It was Ms. Timberlake.
WILMOT: Where did she live?
MARTIN: In San Francisco. I've forgotten what her husband did. Anyway, I would take care of--sort of like a nanny for her little girl. I never missed any time. I just immediately was on strike and then the next day I went to an employment agency and they had that kind of opening. There was a shortage of jobs at that time because the shipyards were over and there were a lot of people looking for work. So I just didn't want to miss a bet, I took that. It's interesting too, I learned a lot.
WILMOT: Were you part of a union at the NBC?
MARTIN: No. I don't think so.
WILMOT: So when the strike happened, you were laid off? Or what happened?
MARTIN: You couldn't work.
WILMOT: You were respecting the--
MARTIN: Yes, somehow, it seemed like it was a strike, it kind of closed down, exactly.
WILMOT: It closed down the company.
MARTIN: Mm-hmm.
WILMOT: Where was NBC located?
MARTIN: On Broadway.
WILMOT: In North Beach?
MARTIN: Yes. On a corner. I've forgotten what is there now. It was about a four-story building.
WILMOT: Did you work with other young black women or was it integrated?
MARTIN: Yes, there were a few blacks and Chinese and Caucasian, others.
WILMOT: I missed when you were telling me the story of how you met your husband, I missed, did you say that the woman who introduced you also worked with you at the NBC?
MARTIN: We worked at the post office together.
WILMOT: Post office, okay, I'm sorry.
MARTIN: It was like a Christmas temp or something like that. That was kind of what they did. She roomed with a lady and they were perfect housekeepers and they did things like dinners and social things. I don't know, I had two of those, maybe that was the second one that she had done for my birthday. That was kind of her thing I guess, with her friends.
WILMOT: Dinner parties and events.
MARTIN: Yes. And then she saw him and she said, Ms. Allison's brother is out there, do you mind if he comes for dinner? I'm not sure if she had that planned for herself or not. After I remembered, when he asked her--well, I didn't think about that very much really, at first. Because I really was--I guess I was so involved with Billy Estine, that I wasn't seeing him or her or anyone. I went to see Billy Estine. He was quite a singer.
WILMOT: What kind of music did he sing?
MARTIN: See, I remember his songs. He had a deep voice. He was fair complected, possibly green eyes or something like that. He's been dead a good while.
WILMOT: I wanted to ask you also, this family that you worked with, where did they stay? Where did they live in San Francisco?
MARTIN: It was on a hill. I learned how to--I always drove, ever since I was a kid, twelve years old I was driving cars and things around the farm, trucks and whatever, wagons, whatever was there, you did it. But I was going up this hill to go to her home, and she would get to the top of the hill, and instead of just putting her foot on the break and holding there she would give the car a little gas, so that the car would hold itself without pulling. So once she started off, she didn't have to try to make the transition off the break to the gas, she was already on it, because there were tremendously steep hills. It was a rich part of the hill, of San Francisco.
WILMOT: Sound like it might have been Russian Hill or Telegraph Hill? In that area.
MARTIN: I don't remember by that particular name. I remember that I watched how she managed those hills. I learned that sometimes you're on a real steep hill and you might roll back, if you move from the break to the gas, and that's the way I could see that she was sure--just as soon as the traffic permitted, all she'd have to do is keep her foot--her feet were still on the gas.
WILMOT: And that was what you learned from her?
MARTIN: Yes. How she would just give it enough gas that the wheel didn't move, just enough. She'd have one foot on the brake and one foot on the gas. I did that the other day, thinking about her. So many other little things I learned about them, working. Because you know, I hadn't been around rich people, wealthy people, those kinds. One had a chocolate company. I stayed with her a while and it didn't work out or something. She offered, she told her friend about me and I went to work for her. Ms. Klein and Ms. Timberlake.
WILMOT: Did you live with her?
MARTIN: Hmm?
WILMOT: Did you live with them while you were taking care of their child?
MARTIN: Oh yes, mm-hmm. I lived in the home. She wanted me to serve the dinner in the evening. She had a cook, but she wanted me to come in and serve it. I remember now that they didn't eat a lot of bread. I think there were two crackers on the plate, on the little dinner plate, the little bread plate, something like that. One of the ladies made a, she cooked a chicken dish in a Dutch oven with sour cream. She still dressed, or got herself up for dinner, and the family came to the table, except the infant. My job was to serve it, but my job really was the baby.
WILMOT: So the baby that you were taking care of with Mrs. Timberlake was an infant?
MARTIN: Both of them had infants.
WILMOT: They were little babies.
MARTIN: That was my job, not the bigger children, just the infant. I was assigned to that. I would bathe the baby and they had a balcony there--after I would finish, while I was doing the chores she would want it out there in the sunshine on the balcony sort of.
WILMOT: The baby?
MARTIN: Mm-hmm.
WILMOT: You hadn't taken care of babies before.
MARTIN: That's right, but she didn't know that. [laughter] I had never put a diaper on a baby. I always laugh about that, I had never put a diaper on a baby before. Because see, my sister was pretty close to me. But I guess I had seen people do it, and you kind of--it was no big thing. I did well with the first one until she gave me to--she had something, something happened in her home, she had to--I don't know. Something happened, maybe--anyway, she got me the job with her friend. Ms. Klein and MS. Timberlake were the two women. I don't know which one I worked for first. I don't even know which one I would watch drive that car and she would keep the gas, just enough gas, the break on and enough gas to sort of keep the car from rolling backward. Somehow she made that connection.
WILMOT: How long did you do that kind of work?
MARTIN: Not too long. Then I think I went back to the NBC company while going to beauty school in San Francisco. I did two things, back at that job. But not while with them, because I was expected to be there night and day, just until you were off. I didn't stay there that long because my job came back and I was finishing my beauty schooling that I had started at Langston.
WILMOT: Did you stay in touch with either of those two women?
MARTIN: No. I left there, back to my job. Then beauty school, then I worked--I can't quite pull it together how I moved from--this is all kind of rapid stuff. You get a job and you're very happy, as I said, jobs were pretty scarce because all these people were relieved from the shipyard. You were kind of happy to get just whatever.
WILMOT: I wanted to ask also, I kind of imagine you as this young person who was making their own money. I wanted to ask, were there special things you liked to buy? Were you into buying clothes?
MARTIN: No.
WILMOT: Or buying sweet things for yourself? Was there anything special you were doing with it? Were you saving to buy a house? Where was your head at at that time?
MARTIN: I think I mentioned that when I first got in, maybe a month or something, I bought these things and took my parents, my dad and mom and my sister--my brother wasn't even home then I don't think--to the beach.
WILMOT: You did mention it, it's beautiful.
MARTIN: I dressed them up. I had bought myself a suit, my mom a suit, and my sister a dress, I remember white with black, and my dad I guess I bought him a suit. We all caught something and we went to San Francisco to that beach. I don't know if you remember, you saw that woman at entrance, she used to be laughing, did you ever see that? She'd just be laughing, just, as you walk up there to enter. It was exciting because she'd be so happy. Everybody kind of remembered that in those days. [Musee Mechanique at the Cliff House/Sutro Baths]
WILMOT: A laughing woman?
MARTIN: Yes, she was a greeter.
WILMOT: That was her job, to laugh and greet people?
MARTIN: It was an imitation woman, it was like a recording, she was just moving her hands, laughing. Just really kind of a--
WILMOT: Like?
MARTIN: Ya-ha-ha-ha! Like the world was just really fine.
WILMOT: Like a mechanical robot?
MARTIN: Yes, something like that. She was up high, and you look, you could just hear her laughter.
WILMOT: Oh my goodness.
MARTIN: And you laugh too, because it brought you, if you went there you would change from a mood of--just laughing too, sort of. She just had a big, just a laugh that would kind of take you from where you are and up to a laughing matter or up to a happy mood.
WILMOT: Did you ever go to the Sutro Baths? Were they open at that time?
MARTIN: The what?
WILMOT: The Sutro Baths. They weren't open then I don't think.
MARTIN: No, I don't know. Most people I think came here was, in those days, was going over there because there were lots of things to do, riding, lots of things to ride. This woman would greet you. I don't know who invented that. She was so happy up there, standing up, making this cheer. She was giving you cheer.
WILMOT: What other things, so you really treated your family, what other things were you doing with your money at that time? After the war were the shortages up--
MARTIN: I put my money in the bank because I didn't have to buy anything at home. My dad and mom bought the groceries I guess, I don't remember buying groceries with my money. I just saved it. That was important to me, that they get acquainted with what's going on around them. I didn't drive, I know that. We took either bus or something to get out there. I was working in San Francisco, so I kind of knew my way around and they hadn't had that experience. I wanted them to enjoy it, and just see people. That's why I thought they should be dressed up because they were going to meet people, new experiences, and not just to go to the beach, with bathing suits. They were just going to sightsee.
WILMOT: When did you start to move into real estate?
MARTIN: The first thing I can remember, the first venture that I'd like to have had was when I asked Dad to buy that place for his store. I always think about, he let that woman convince him and he had no faith in me. I guess I was young, you know. I might have been nineteen or maybe just twenty, something like that. I was also going to beauty school and I would come back and they would be working at that store, the little Kiss Shack. I would start dipping that ice cream, because people would line up for ice cream, until my finger started turning, because you would be--
WILMOT: You were telling me about that last time and I didn't understand what you meant.
MARTIN: You know an ice cream dipper?
WILMOT: Mm-hmm.
MARTIN: Well, you just keep dipping it. And you dip so much in a--you'd have to get something from the freezer that was still hard again, very difficult.
WILMOT: And it started to shape your finger.
MARTIN: It was just like that, because you're dipping. I would come up after getting home from work and work with them. I still stayed at, I still lived at home with them and I didn't buy anything. Whatever money I made was--you know, I didn't take care of the household.
WILMOT: It was your money.
MARTIN: Mm-hmm. But I guess I really more than worked for it too, in a sense. Not that I was thinking that, but I just knew that it was a lot of work, to manage the amount of impact that little village had. Anything else was far away. Not far away, but they could just walk right in the neighborhood and run up and get--it finally got to be certain things like greens.
WILMOT: You said that, that eventually your father started selling greens in the store.
MARTIN: In that little thing, and some other things. Whatever people, popular demand. Some bread and things of that, he was selling--what you more or less call vegetables of some sort. Black people, the people that inhabited that part of that was quite a bit of black people and they loved those--they still like greens. It's like quite a delicacy for black people to eat mustard and turnips and collards, and collards especially. They grow them in gardens. I understand that they found out that why they ate scraps, historically, and they had collard greens, and it had all the--it's such a healthy food to eat. Some other items were just exactly what they could get. I kind of think that's one of God's mysteries that this is what they could get, was collard greens.
WILMOT: But it was very healthy.
MARTIN: It's very healthy, yes. And beans too. Something they ate. I think beans are still really good now, if you don't put a lot of meat with it. I think if you eat beans you should limit your meat, in my way of thinking.
WILMOT: That's what a lot of vegetarians do.
MARTIN: Beans?
WILMOT: Mm-hmm. As substitute for meat.
MARTIN: Yes, they have a lot of protein, huh? I thought about that when I heard a speaker speak about that. It was a political woman, a black lady, and she was saying how that the scraps--the lord will always sort of--things will really work for you and you don't know how it comes about, but there you had limited choices of food, but what they were eating was very healthy for you. Have you eaten any greens? We say greens and that means normally turnips, mustard, collards. Like Swiss chard no, that wouldn't be in it, and cabbage.
WILMOT: They're in my yard, I grow them.
MARTIN: Which one?
WILMOT: Collard.
MARTIN: Do you really?
WILMOT: Mm-hmm.
MARTIN: Oh really? Do you eat them?
WILMOT: Mm-hmm. I have to. They get so big, you have to chop them down and they just come back, again, they're magic.
MARTIN: Yes.
WILMOT: So that experience with your father's store was kind of the first time that you were speculating about real estate and thinking about, "Oh, this is a good deal." When did you really start to enter the field?
MARTIN: I think the first property I went after was when my husband and I were going to get married and I told him we should get a house first, and if he'd get the money from his father we'd pay him back, and he borrowed the money. It's like, segregation too, because they put a little cross in our yard, we were the first black to buy on Twenty-second and Potrero.
WILMOT: This was where in Richmond?
MARTIN: Yes.
WILMOT: Can you tell me about that house?
MARTIN: It's still there, it's Twenty-second and Potrero, it was a three bedroom and we paid $2000 down that we borrowed from his father and then we had the baby pretty quickly, and there we had a three bedroom and a baby. I guess we still had this Plymouth car that he had. Then we paid his father off in a year. Then I worked for the government for two years, for Benecia. I was a sample selector for commercial contractors. I was given a truck, a pickup, to go out to the different places in the arsenal, the Benecia arsenal, to find products that were bought but they were just in storage, and there was no preservative or packaging for them. It was like pistons and high precision material items that were going to be wasted or destroyed, because of the condensation and so forth. I would have a laborer to go with me. Because you'd have to understand all metals, all fabrics, to know what preservative and packaging was necessary, for an export shipment or long storage.
WILMOT: Wow, you became an expert.
MARTIN: Yes, I went to class there. I took the test to be a--a special test to be a licensed driver on the arsenal. I had a driver's license but I had to go to school there. Then I was expected to go out to all these storage places and go in and have a sample taken out of whatever was stored there. I had a laborer with me and they would open it, and then I would divide it up, the quantity, how many were there. It was also a way of finding out really what they had, I think. Then we would either ship it off, here, some of it came to Richmond where a private contractor would preserve and package it. Like for example, a break shoe, things like that, was handled different than pistons, I would say, or any high-precision metal item, we packaged different. Then we had to pretty much all dip it in the packaging part, I worked in that department as well, where when you package it, there was a process of a wax-like wrapping paper, and then it was dipped into something that melted plastic, something like that, that you dipped this package in. Especially on highly precision items, because they wouldn't be any good, if they got to war, and they pulled that out and it had rust or deterioration, it wouldn't function well. So we had put extreme protection on that. That's why, I guess, you have to go to school to be able to make sure you know these items and these fabrics.
WILMOT: As I understand then, you were dealing with the post-war stockpile of things. Is that right or wrong?
MARTIN: Yes. The war was over, but then they were still--I think due to the rush probably they a lot of things just shipped in, because it was one prerequisite that we make sure that we knew the quantity there, and the location, the building.
WILMOT: You were doing the--the inventory as well.
MARTIN: Sort of so, yes, and then what did it need? Most everything had to be pulled out and shipped, either on the arsenal there that we had packaging and preserving sections.
WILMOT: Where was it shipped to?
MARTIN: It was shipped from the warehouses and things from around there, brought into the location where they had all the preservative and packaging material.
WILMOT: Were you the only person doing that work, or did you have other counterparts?
MARTIN: There were two of us. There was a girl that looked similar to you, Jane Matthews, was my assistant.
WILMOT: She was.
MARTIN: She wasn't my assistant, she had the same thing I had.
WILMOT: She was your counterpart.
MARTIN: She was assigned a pickup and a person laborer to check things for you.
WILMOT: You and your colleague, you were both women, and it sounds to me like this was not traditionally women's work. It sounds like it was very mechanical, am I wrong? I'm wondering, did you ever encounter any attitudes about being women from other people who were at the arsenal, or at the warehouses?
MARTIN: Well, you know, I think--your question is well taken, but I think it goes back to, women had been doing welding and all of that. See, this was after the war. I didn't work in shipyards and see all that. But I think it was already, people were conditioned now from women stepping out, doing all kinds of things, during World War II. Even at the ammunitions depot in McAllister where I worked before going to college, women were there working. Here in the shipyards they were working, and it really--men were gone to the service and the women were working. Benecia was just a continuation. People had all gotten acclimated to that kind of thing. I had no problem with the men that I dealt with. I had been used to that, as a child, working with people, men.
WILMOT: Working with men, yes.
MARTIN: Yes, because that was my job, I was always right there with my dad and my brother, and my neighbor.
WILMOT: The men you worked with, did they have no problem with you as well?
MARTIN: I didn't have any problem at all. They were my laborer, there to find what I wanted to know, how many, the quantity and open the package, however it was packaged--usually it was just in a pasteboard, cardboard type of packaging, which was not sufficient to even stay there a long time, if you leave it out a long time, condensation and other things would destroy it.
WILMOT: How did you learn about that job?
MARTIN: Well, people were going--people were getting hired to go to Benecia, in the area, and somehow I applied and got the job there two. It was about a two-year job.
WILMOT: Were you a young mom at that time? Or did you have your baby after that job?
MARTIN: I had the baby, but the baby was kept by my mom and dad. I had to, you know, they had been moved, transitioned from Canal to the upper end, where I met my first husband. They were taking down Canal, down this way, destroying, taking that down. I think taking that down and then they were moving people up higher on either Potrero or Cutting, out that way.
WILMOT: The other thing is, as a young woman, this was your first baby, was this a daughter or a son?
MARTIN: Daughter.
WILMOT: Her name?
MARTIN: Gloria Loretta.
WILMOT: Who did you name her for?
MARTIN: I just liked the name Gloria. Loretta, I don't remember, I think there was Loretta Young on the TV or something like that, I thought it was a pretty name.
WILMOT: Did you like being pregnant? Were you sick a lot? Did you enjoy being pregnant?
MARTIN: I was fine until two weeks before she was born. I thought she was going to be born and she wasn't. I stayed in the hospital a day or two and then they sent me home. I guess, you know, she weighed nine and a half pounds, so she was a pretty big child. But she didn't ever hold that. She's short, she's about your height, maybe about your size.
WILMOT: Was the labor difficult or easy?
MARTIN: I thought I was--I told the lady, "Turn me loose! Let me die!" [laughter] This real large lady was holding my hands, and it looked to me like she was making it worse. If I could just have my hands free, but she was holding my hands back like that. She cried out when she came and I guess I slept right after that. It was in San Francisco, most of the babies at that time was born over at Stanford Lane, something like that, there were many children in my age level, children were born there. They had a, I guess, Stanford must have had that together. I probably didn't know much about Stanford, but I remember--I guess it was a part of that same Stanford, had this maternity ward there. I met a girl from Samora? Samoran woman.
WILMOT: Somoa?
MARTIN: Samoan, yes. She was there with me.
WILMOT: Having a baby too or as a nurse?
MARTIN: No, because there was a lot of us in the same room, like six people.
WILMOT: Did you stay long in the hospital?
MARTIN: No, I didn't stay--well, they kept you longer than they do now. I talked about her country, her culture, with her a lot. Some people in the room might--I didn't remember them. But I remember discussing her country. I think she was almost as dark complected as me, and from another country. And her baby probably was darker than my child, and she wasn't a black person. Something kind of like that, she and I started talking and sharing culture. Especially culture. I had no information on that country. I don't remember anyone else in the room. I remember my sister coming with my coat on to see the baby. She didn't have--she married before I did, but she didn't have any children. She came over. I had a rose colored coat, it was new, and she had stopped by my house, and I guess she was dressing up to come to the hospital to see the baby. [laughter] She was an only child. Gloria was, is, an only child.
WILMOT: Was your husband comfortable with you working? Was he comfortable with there being two providers? First I should ask, what kind of work did he do?
MARTIN: He worked at American Standard Radiator. Which was a plant that made bathtubs. His brother in law was working there when he came out of service. He worked there until he got injured. He was injured.
WILMOT: So he was there for many years?
MARTIN: Probably so, yes. At least, let me see. I guess fifteen years, and then he was injured. He was in an accident, not on the job.
WILMOT: One second, I want to get something, because I'm worried about your glasses that are right here.
MARTIN: Oh, thanks. [pause]
WILMOT: I wanted to ask you about being a young mother and balancing parenting with working and everything that you had going on.
MARTIN: Well, I did the two years at Benecia, naturally my mom and dad kept the baby.
WILMOT: You just made a funny face to me, like I asked a funny question. [laughter]
MARTIN: I was thinking about something, about what I was going to tell you later. That was, the child was on these, like when I was relocating houses, she was on all of those sites, she'd been everywhere. She was just with me everywhere I went. I remember in auctions she'd be at the auction place with me, you just take your baby with you. I do that now, because I'll have my grandchildren here and I'll be--I was doing a house in North Richmond I guess about five years ago, and this granddaughter of mine was always up at the permit place in Martinez or on the job site with me, or in her school. If she was at school I'd pick her up and then go where I had to go. That was the same with their mother's life. Whatever I was doing, she would--you know, if she wasn't at school, she was subject to being with me. Rather than just sitting with a babysitter. I didn't probably have the money to do a babysitting thing. So they just went. I remember when I bought one house, I had just bought her--had her made, I think--a coat. It was so pretty, a little casual coat. I don't know where I got that coat. Maybe I didn't get it made. I remember this little coat and people liked it. I did too. It was just a simple coat. It was like, little checks, I don't even know how it was, but I thought it was nice. It wasn't like a red coat or a brown coat, it was little checks with maybe, maybe with a collar, a little velvet collar on it or something, black velvet. I can't quite remember it. I can remember taking her with me on where I had to go. I was not saying, "I'm staying here with this child today," I'm saying, "We're going to work, you're going where I go."
WILMOT: So she grew up on the job with you.
MARTIN: Yes. I had a real estate office in a grocery store, deli store, on Seventh Street which still is there now. I moved from the home that we had bought, we moved above the store, and we stayed there while I ran the store down below. So she was there. All of her little cousins, my sister's children, would love to come and stay in my house. On the weekends, I could see them coming over. The drivers of my brother in law's trucks would come past my store, and some cashed checks there, and the little girl, my sister's little girl or boy would catch a ride with their father's drivers to get to my house, especially on Fridays. They liked to be there. It was really an easy way to work, because I met so many people, and friends would be coming, and they would actually be buying from my store. Some people would get a new car and they'd drive up so I could see it. I enjoyed that work.
WILMOT: Sounds like you were popular. [laughter]
MARTIN: Well, I wasn't popular. [laughter] Because popular means different than there laboring, that was work. I opened the store at seven in the morning. I didn't go to get that store. What happened, I was working on a relocation property, used to be Cadillac Ambulance, had a, I guess, just starting an ambulance business. They had a big garage where they kept the ambulances here. They sold it and I think they went to probably where they are now. They moved. The house was in the way of the freeway, those were the freeway houses I was dealing with. I was there working on one on Seventh and Ohio, which my daughter has now, Gloria still has the house. I was down covering the floor to paint, to do something, and I picked up a newspaper and it said, "Auction for store equipment today," that particular day at 1:00 or 12:00. I remember, I wanted to go, I knew I had bought this building that had the store, zoned for a store, used to be one. I went there and bought all the equipment, at an auction, you know? Seemed like no one came to bid but me. I bid on this stuff. Then I had to move it. My dad helped me move it. It was interesting how he took big two inch pipe and would roll these things, these big refrigerators, large size.
WILMOT: On a platform, he would roll the whole platform?
MARTIN: No, he would put this roller under it and then he'd let it roll down, from up in the truck. He and I pretty much moved all that stuff out of this building. It was on Twelfth Street near here. This is Tenth, it was right over there, close to Social Security, close here. We brought it all in and I had, for little or nothing, I had bought myself--you know, the store was put together for a very small amount of money, a full store. With all the stuff I brought back, bought very inexpensively. I brought it there and placed it in there and then opened that store up. I was going to move in that house, it was part of what the ambulance people had, for the real estate office. So I separated the corner. I used to know the owner of every vacant land in Richmond, that's later, so I know where the sites were, what sites were available and what people had.
WILMOT: Let's go back a little bit. You were telling me, I remember you mentioning that when you moved into that first house with Mr. Petgrave, the three bedroom house on Twenty-sixth?
MARTIN: Second, and Potrero.
WILMOT: Twenty-second and Potrero. You said you were the first black family in that neighborhood. Who was in that neighborhood at that time?
MARTIN: Caucasian. They built houses and they bought all of those. It was a popular area for them.
WILMOT: Were they white people who were from--were they Italian, Portuguese?
MARTIN: I think they were probably a lot of the shipyard workers.
WILMOT: So they were from the Midwest and the South, white people from the Midwest and the South.
MARTIN: Mostly that I think, really, from the South, that were our neighbors.
WILMOT: You said they put a cross on your yard?
MARTIN: One morning there was a cross there.
WILMOT: In the beginning, right after you bought it?
MARTIN: Yes. But you know what gave me a lot of strength, my husband didn't have any fear. He just took it down and kept going, it was just like that. He didn't have any fear about it. He was kind of that strong-willed person anyway. Then again, he probably--he probably couldn't--I don't know. I remember him just kind of taking it down and destroying it, and kept going where he was going. Like he had no fear, he didn't express to me, that he pretty much couldn't care, wasn't scared. I kind of thought about it, but his strength, the way he handled it, it just was nothing. It was just, some prankster put the thing up there, seems the way he thought.
WILMOT: It was a cross, it was just a cross?
MARTIN: It was a cross, but they had done that out in another, some time ago, there was a black man--somewhere during that particular time in there, after then--I don't believe he knew what it meant until Mr. Gary some years later bought a home out in the San Pablo area and they put a cross and there was a lot made of that cross. But I had seen one and we didn't react to it, and it seemed to be nothing.
WILMOT: And your neighbors, did the neighborhood change rapidly from white to black, or did it stay white?
MARTIN: It stayed white a while, and then it started turning black.
WILMOT: Where were your neighbors moving to?
MARTIN: In the direction of growth of the city. Normally, you know, it's been a pattern that the whites get the house, and then when the new ones come, they sell that to less fortunate and they move to others, in each district. It's been a pattern that way, they move, possibly San Pablo or Hilltop, or something like that. Possibly Hilltop.
WILMOT: Did you later become friends with your neighbors? Were you ever friends with them?
