MARTIN: There was a woman called Dixie Lee and her mother, reared some kids next door and they were friendly. Then someone lived in front of us, I don't know that that friendly, but we got along okay.
WILMOT: Were you worried about your daughter growing up in that neighborhood?
MARTIN: No. I said my husband in the beginning, he had no fear. He just took the thing and threw it away. I guess he took it as a prankster or something. His strength in that, I wasn't scared. Maybe I really wasn't too much involved until some segregation started here. I think it was, they burned the cross or something over at--they didn't burn this one--over at Misty Garets--their family bought a house over in some part of San Pablo and they either burned the cross, something like that. Maybe that's when the fear finally came. In my husband's case, at our house, he just destroyed it and went on to work.
WILMOT: How much was that house when you first bought it?
MARTIN: I don't even know how much it was, because later on houses like that were selling for $6,000, but evidently that one was, maybe $4,000.
WILMOT: You put half down and then the other $2,000 you had as a loan.
MARTIN: I think we got an FHA or something like that.
WILMOT: And who was your broker? Did you go through a broker?
MARTIN: I don't remember if we went through a broker or not. You know, again, that was my husband's area. All of his life he lived in that same district, on Hoffman Boulevard, it just like, at Sixteenth Street and that was Twenty-second Street. That too, he probably just felt pretty strongly about that was his neighborhood, or nothing was going on. Before they'd built those homes he was already here on Hoffman, you know, where his father bought on Hoffman. Before that, that was nothing but vacant land. That was his early experience of that area. I think it was right after the war. I think it's like when those babies coming home, all over the world, we'll have the wedding rings and things, and sweethearts will sing. All of that was happening, it was coming together, they were home again, they were getting these baby boomers and the house was cheap, inexpensive. But that was a lot of money too, in a sense. I think at one of my jobs I was making thirty dollars a week. You probably can compute it out, now. From $10.75 minimum wage, six something up to what, instead of a week, some people make thirty dollars a day. That was my thinking when we were speaking of getting married is that we should get us a house. When we bought the house he stayed there before I did. He stayed there by himself until we got married, and then I came into the home. I already, we were getting everything together, as far as an idea of what we were going to do. From that house to other houses.
WILMOT: I wanted to ask you one other question about this. In that timeframe, what did Richmond look like in terms of race, who lived where?
MARTIN: North Richmond is where we first lived, it's where the blacks lived. Except the older black families, which probably was thirty families, which was the Petgrave family and the Harris family, was a woman that lived across the street.
WILMOT: People who had been there for a while?
MARTIN: Yes, they were the first blacks--they came over with Richmond, if you speak to some older-time black, Caucasian people around, they can recall the Ellisons, the Petgraves, the Blackmans, Ms. Harris and her son. The Bonaparts. They just know, they can name them probably. Not over twenty families before the war. They were spread out I think, but they were in North Richmond as well then. That family lived in South Richmond, my husband's family. I don't know, there were more black people from the shipyard, I think they landed with--the shipyards, they came to North Richmond like we did, as a place that we could find a place.
WILMOT: Can we take a break?
MARTIN: Yes.
[interview interruption]
WILMOT: Okay, we just took a break, thank you.
MARTIN: How many pages--you got it on now?
WILMOT: How many?
MARTIN: Is it on?
WILMOT: Mm-hmm.
MARTIN: How many pages will your document be?
WILMOT: At this time around fifty.
MARTIN: Pages? Of what I've said?
WILMOT: Mm-hmm.
MARTIN: Would that take--did I do that many words?
WILMOT: Mm-hmm.
MARTIN: Really?
WILMOT: Fifty, maybe twenty-five. I think it'll be between thirty and fifty by the time we're done. At least that. I have a question for you. You had started talking to me about the way Richmond had been before the war in terms of residential, different neighborhoods and who lived where in terms of race and ethnicity. I was wondering, after the war, you mentioned that there were the first families, African American families who scattered throughout, maybe twenty, maybe less, maybe a little more. After the war, how did the terrain change here in Richmond? Where did the southern whites who had migrated here, where did they settle? Where did the southern blacks who had migrated here, where did they settle? How did it look?
MARTIN: After the war--this is 1949 is when we bought the house in the so-called "white neighborhood" at this time. Those were relatively new houses. The time that that started happening, the transition, the whites were going to San Pablo and Hilltop I think. Moving away. [sneezes]
WILMOT: Bless you.
MARTIN: The whites were buying in that which we call the directional growth. The blacks were taking the south side over. Transitioned from North Richmond to South Richmond, sort of went that way, so San Pablo and Hilltop was basically a white district. Finally the blacks moved in pretty rapidly over there on Twenty-second Street and Potrero and over to Hoffman and the Hoffman area.
WILMOT: What year was that?
MARTIN: It started--we started there in about '49, '50, somewhere in there.
WILMOT: So shortly after your family moved in there.
MARTIN: Yes. It doesn't seem like it was not that short--
WILMOT: Five years?
MARTIN: No, no, say, in another year, it was. People were really buying homes. It was sort of like now, the government is into suggesting ways to get home ownership right now, building up a lot of houses. That's what happened after the war. They've done that, I've see this kind of building up houses. I've seen it at least three times when the government gets involved. I think they build the economy on house-building. It's one of the industries that helps people from having--time when there's nothing to do, when there's no jobs--they use housing as a stimulant to bring some economy to the area.
WILMOT: When you started, that was your first house, and that was your home. That was your family home. Was there someone who you learned about real estate from?
MARTIN: I can't quite figure it out no more than I could figure out--no one ever told me that Dad should build that store and he should just go to Martinez. I don't remember anyone telling me. I probably asked someone where you go and they tell me Martinez, and I said, "Dad, let's go to Martinez and buy."
WILMOT: And see about who owns the property.
MARTIN: Yes, because my mom was a very thrifty person and she had, I think, even though the drought, she brought some money to California. Then she worked at McAllister, Oklahoma, at that ammunition plant. So she had money and could have bought it. It was Daddy's decision. She probably, if I was speaking to her, she might have--
WILMOT: Worked on it.
MARTIN: Worked on it, but then she probably didn't have what it took to work on it when she asked him not to stop work and set up the little market that he did set up.
WILMOT: That was her idea?
MARTIN: It was his idea, and she didn't want him to do it. She thought that he would do well to stay on in the store, I mean, in the construction yard where he was.
WILMOT: So he was more--
MARTIN: See, he had left--it was some kind of government clean-up at that section which was not very far from the shipyard that was part of his construction work that he was doing, that he noticed this little building that he thought--and I can't imagine how he dreamed upon that, but that's what he came up with. I came out of that thinking that you ought to have your own store.
WILMOT: That plus your family had this whole history of land ownership in Oklahoma, which you've talked about many times.
MARTIN: Probably so, you never know what you'll have. I found out more talking about myself talking to you than I knew. Things, events, and how things work, and have worked in my life, and that I never really thought about. Much of it I had, but some of it, becomes kind of clear, why my mom was able to keep her land. I was talking to you and you said, "Why is that?" And I thought, well, and I thought about she had gone to school and she had an education, and she had had some experiences I'm sure with what her colleagues said to her, how stupid the natives were. She asked the woman, "How can you tell them?" She said, "Oh really? How can you tell them?" She being a native person, evidently, at that time she was different. This woman thinks she's like her, an out of stater, like the state people. I think they were thinking--I don't know what that woman was thinking or why she said it, but she had had some experiences with other natives, but something about my mother's conduct, you know she didn't think she was one, she's telling how stupid they were.
WILMOT: I know. One thing I didn't ask you on tape was what your parents looked like.
MARTIN: In appearance?
WILMOT: Mm-hmm.
MARTIN: My mother was very much an African looking woman, very African. Then my father was, had been mistaken for Indian in Albuquerque on the way here to California, when we were making our transition from Oklahoma here. I still had some money left and I wanted Indian jewelry, so we were in Flagstaff, Arizona, and I couldn't wait to get out of the car. My dad was a coffee drinker, and he said his head was hurting because he wanted some coffee. Then we stopped at this place, it had a little general store with Indian jewelry and I rushed to get in there, I just wanted this Indian jewelry. My mother came in and my sister, I think all of us kind of wanted to see other things, except we didn't really want any coffee. I guess we were going to eat. We went in and I got a piece of Indian jewelry, my mother got snacks for the ride, and I don't know what my sister got. Then we went to go in the restaurant part and the woman raised her hand, "Oh no, no! We don't serve blacks here!" My dad was sitting up there eating and he looked back at us. [laughter] It was quite comical.
Of course, my mother and we just turned around and walked out. Then Dad, he had just about finished eating, because we spent, I don't know, thirty minutes or so there with that jewelry. I remember, her hands went up just to block us. We weren't going to--of course we already knew about segregation, black and white thing. But my dad, I don't think he thought. He went in and being just like other people--Arizona people--my dad might have been fairer than they were and had straight hair--my father didn't have curl in his hair. His hair was just straight black hair when I first knew him, then straight white hair upon death, at the time he passed. That was quite an event. Of course, it was just like, "Okay," and we backed off.
My mother would always back off anyway. I might would challenge something maybe. I never did though, because I had the same experience at Langston when I left Oklahoma, going to Langston. We got to Tulsa and then there was, like, Oklahoma A and M children were all going to school too, and coming in from these areas, all moving up to go up to A and M or Langston, and maybe other colleges, I don't know, wherever they were going to school and that was the basic ones. It was fine, in a sort, I think, but when we got to Tulsa to catch another bus they said, "Only black on the back seat." It was a lot of children, waiting, and they filled it up with the Caucasian, none other than black, and only gave us the back seat. They would allow us to sit as close as we could, or however we managed, we had to manage the back seat, and wait for the next bus. All the whites got on. I think I was able to get on that first bus, or maybe I didn't, I don't quite remember. We didn't make a scene, we just obeyed that. I can remember it. It was just the way it was. You haven't been taught, necessarily, to fight, say anything. Well, you know, it was pretty much the same way until Ms. Rosa Parks decided she was not going to sit on the back seat. That's why it became such an issue, such a noble thing that she did, because we were just kind of obeying it, the black people were just--like the woman in Flagstaff saying, "Go!" We just, we go, we go out.
WILMOT: Plus there was the idea that there could be consequences. At that time--I think it's hard for our generation to understand, but at that time, there was fear. You could--disobeying that kind of segregation could result in some kind of harm or fear. I don't think it's a matter of--
MARTIN: I know my mother would, she was, she feared things, and I'm certain--she didn't have to but we were just like her, we just backed off. Dad, I remember looking at my dad looking back [laughter], looking at his family. He never mentioned that, even though he was--I'm sure a lot mistaken for Indian. I never heard him use the word, anything, that he looked like, as being an advantage for him. I never heard him use the word "black." You know, sometimes black people talk to each other and talk about, "Oh, that person is black," meaning darker darker skin, and that light one is such--it's been a thing that the light people had more of an advantage than the darker skin. They probably do, because in my dad's case, he was able to drink his coffee and eat his breakfast and we couldn't. When you read it and hear it and when you lived if like I have. I saw that and I was a part thereof, of my own father eating and we couldn't. Of course, getting on that bus, the black children were obedient.
WILMOT: You were children.
MARTIN: Yes, well, we were kind of old, high school graduates and things. Probably my brother and all them in service ran into those things and they just accepted them as--you know, that's the way it is, it's no biggie.
WILMOT: Well, when you came to the Bay Area, did you find that attitudes and practices around race and race discrimination, segregation were similar to what you had grown up with, or did you find things to be very different here, or not very different?
MARTIN: Well, you know, I was thinking about when I worked at the National Biscuit, the NBC company in San Francisco, on Broadway and something there, that I became a supervisor. The supervisor would be the relief person, because it was a kind of an automated thing and you had to be actually synchronized with time, otherwise the product would come and log in, it wouldn't keep flowing, if you couldn't manage the weight checking, the different things you had to do, the carton formers, that hand-formed the cartons and put them on a belt. If you couldn't keep up then it would jam the whole thing. The bakers upstairs were constantly making their product and it was flowing downward, and at that level we had to get it packaged, weight checked and packaged. It was interesting, it would shoot down a pound or whatever size we were doing, whatever size we were packaging, it would fall. But then there would be sometimes a little more or something, we'd have to check every one of those. You couldn't let it jam, you had to keep moving. That's the way I got to be assistant to--I wasn't the exact supervisor, but her assistant--because I could keep up, whatever it was you had to keep it moving. You couldn't have a carton--make the boxes that they're using, the packages, they had to have enough on the line all the time to meet the weight checking person. That person had to adjust the weight, add a little or whatever it was, to make it a pound or whatever amount of weight that it should be. The others had to take it and package it. It had to continue to flow, that's how I got that job, I could manage to relieve everybody. That's all I did was relieve them--
WILMOT: You were a floater.
MARTIN: Yes.
WILMOT: You could float.
MARTIN: Yes.
WILMOT: Let's stop for one minute. I'm either going to change the media or we're going to end for today and I'm going to ask you about that.
MARTIN: Oh okay. [pause]
WILMOT: This one, and we're not going to film this part but we might need to go back to it. I had asked you a question about residential, but how did you experience racial discrimination here, versus Oklahoma? I had wanted to ask you--[pause] You had started telling me a story about NBC in response to that question. I wanted to ask you more about what you experienced when you got here.
MARTIN: When I came to California as far as racial segregation?
WILMOT: Or discrimination, racism.
MARTIN: Discrimination. I knew that my father's friend that was living out here was in, well, right away we were in a black community, which was North Richmond. North Richmond was a unique part of the city that black people had. It was like Las Vegas, like a little miniature Las Vegas. Every block had a black business. They were really entrepreneurs. They had beauty shops, they owned the little houses, and they built the houses with their hands, they kind of piggy-backed together. My brother in law had a friend called Pep Martin, real good cement man, plaster man. Somebody else, Booker Adger, was also a stucco person.
WILMOT: Adger?
MARTIN: Adger, Booker Adger. Plumbers, they knew how to do all these things and they would build their own buildings, many of them. They would either build them, or--I think those were some buildings, I think where we lived was an old building that had previously been owned by a white. This woman had one room that we stopped in there. They had another man, Mr. Frazier, had taken concrete that you ship up to do concrete work, and he made a hotel called the Frazier Hotel in North Richmond. There was people that had little nightclubs, various nightclubs, they had a lot of nightclubs. I don't think they have any there now. There was all kinds of them, the Savoy, the Downbeat, the Tapper's Inn, the {Bregis Place?}, all these places after like some big city. They were all functioning and crowds of people, but the people I think, many of them came from Oakland, San Francisco, all would come to North Richmond. As a matter of fact, they are asking now how to do this Main Street, what made such an impact, how there were people--there were some musicians who were fairly renown would come to what they called Tapper's Inn to dances and things like that. T-Bone Walker, Iva Joe Hunter, and I don't know who else was coming to North Richmond to entertain. Then all at once, now that's all gone, right now, it's gone almost 90 percent, at least 80 percent of Richmond is now, it's turned to another nationality, it's basically Spanish.
WILMOT: Mexican?
MARTIN: Whereas the blacks have kind of integrated throughout, since integration. That heritage they had has been lost because it's pretty much Spanish community now. It's moving fast, very rapidly.
WILMOT: What was going on in Point Richmond? Was that always--
MARTIN: Point Richmond. I don't know. Other than later, when I realized there was a Point Richmond, we used to go to the Plunge.
WILMOT: Did you go?
MARTIN: Yes, we'd go to the Plunge down there. Now, it's still there, but I think they use the funds to do one up at Kennedy School, rather than to do money that was appropriate. There was a fight between putting the money up at Kennedy High School over there on Cutting, or either placing it in the old Plunge, Richmond Plunge. They probably will get to it later, but it's still standing there. I don't think it's usable right now.
WILMOT: I think we should close for today.
MARTIN: I can tell you one thing about Point Richmond. They had one businessman opened up Dugan's Chicken Kitchen right there at the railroad track there in Point Richmond, had a very successful business. He had rooms above. That's some of the older black people who were entrepreneurs in doing things like that. They seem like they came with that strength that right now, I don't believe we had the strength that we had then, as far as doing a lot of thing that they did then. Everybody was doing, trying to build their own house, or build their own business, or doing things more than--seems like now they're moving into other peoples' projects, in other words, jobs.
WILMOT: Working for people as opposed to working for themselves.
MARTIN: Yes. But they had no choice at that time, I think, to make things move. In their little churches--my church is the oldest church, I think, black church, in this area, one of the oldest.
WILMOT: In North Richmond or in the Bay Area?
MARTIN: Richmond. We came from--North Oakland is in Oakland, it's older. But that's the sister or brother, sister church to our church. They came out here and helped to continue, to build the first North Richmond Baptist church.
WILMOT: That's your church? That's where you've been going since you moved here?
MARTIN: Yes. In California, yes.
WILMOT: And they came from a church in North Oakland called?
MARTIN: North Oakland Baptist. So we fellowship closely together now.
WILMOT: North Oakland and then Richmond, North Richmond.
MARTIN: Uh-huh.
WILMOT: Well, I want to ask you about that and many other things the next time speak, and I look forward to it.
MARTIN: Oh, okay. It's a lot of interesting things that you pull out of me that I didn't really put together. Like, I hear it now, it was just something that happened and gone on, but now I'm able to kind of think about things like what really went on, and what's still going on, really. Anyway, it's a joy, because you have that pleasing way of asking me things and it's so calm I can kind of think about things that I had never thought seriously, in that light, just moving along.
WILMOT: I think your voice and your experiences are very, very valuable and important for history.
MARTIN: Really? It's strange, I really didn't think about--some few things you brought out of me that, oh, wait a minute, I hadn't thought about, you move on with your life.
[End of Session]
